content.full_text:
RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
May 3, 1996


Raymond R. Holland
Private Industry Council of
San Francisco, Inc.,
745 Franklin Street, Suite 400
San Francisco, CA 94102-3117

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-96-102

Dear Mr. Holland:

This is in response to your request for assistance under the
conflict-of-interest disclosure provisions of the Political Reform
Act (the "Act").1/ Since you are merely asking for confirmation
or clarification of the Act's disclosure provisions, we are
treating your letter as a request for informal assistance.2/

QUESTIONS

1. Does the Act require disclosure of business positions
held in a nonprofit or charitable organization?

2. On an annual statement of economic interests filed for
the 1995 calendar year, do public officials report interests
acquired or disposed of after January 1, 1996?

CONCLUSIONS

1. The Act requires public officials designated in an
agency's conflict-of-interest code to disclose positions in
"business entities." A "business entity" is defined as an
organization or enterprise operated for profit. Accordingly,

_________________
1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections 18000-18954. All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2/ Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Government Code Section 83114; 2 Cal. Code of Regs. Section
18329(c)(3).)

428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814 (916) 322-5660

Advice File No. I-96-102
Page 2


disclosure of a position held in a nonprofit organization or a
charitable organization is not required under the Act.

2. Annual statements of economic interests reflect the
interests held by a public official in the prior calendar year.
Any interests acquired or disposed of after December 31 of that
calendar year would be reported on the official's next statement.

FACTS

You are the vice-president of Planning and Legal Affairs for
the Private Industry Council of San Francisco, Inc., a government
agency (the "PIC"). Many of the members of the PIC are
representatives of private, nonprofit organizations or nonprofit
public benefit corporations.

When you distributed the 1995-96 Form 730 - Statement of
Economic Interests to the members of the PIC for completion, you
obliterated the entire second paragraph of the instruction for
Schedule G, the schedule used to report business positions. That
paragraph reads: "Positions held in nonprofit or charitable
organizations or campaign committees are not reportable."
(Schedule G of Form 730, Statement of Economic Interests.) You
deleted this instruction because you believe it to be inconsistent
with the letter, if not the spirit, of the Political Reform Act.

ANALYSIS

The Act requires every governmental agency to promulgate a
conflict-of-interest code. A conflict-of-interest code is a rule
or regulation which specifically enumerates positions within the
agency which make, participate in making, or use their official
position to influence decisions. The code also describes the
specific types of investments, business positions, interests in
real property and sources of income to be reported. (Section
87300, et seq.)

An investment, business position, interest in real property,
or source of income will be reportable under the code if the
business entity in which the investment or business position is
held, the interest in real property, or the income or source of
income may foreseeably be affected materially by any decision made
or participated in by the designated employee by virtue of his or
her position. (Section 87302(a).) The definition of "business
entity" as used above means any organization or enterprise
operated for profit. (Section 82005.)

Advice File No. I-96-102
Page 3


     Accordingly, if you have a member who holds a position as an
employee, director or officer of a nonprofit organization, or a
nonprofit public benefit corporation, that position is not
reportable.3/

     Public officials disclose financial interests at specified
times as outlined by the conflict-of-interest code. For instance,
the code also requires that new officials of an agency file an
assuming office statement within 30 days of assuming office. When
an official terminates his or her position, a leaving office
statement must be filed within 30 days of leaving. In addition,
public officials file statements of economic interests on an
annual basis. In addition to specifying when statements must be
filed, the conflict-of-interest code also specifies the period of
time that financial interests must be reported. For instance on
the assuming office statement, a public official must disclose
investments, business positions and interests in real property
held on the date he or she assumes office and income received in
the 12 months before assuming office.

     On the annual statement of economic interests, public
officials disclose investments, business positions and real
property held, and income received, in the prior calendar year.
A public official who acquires or disposes of a particular
interest after the last day of the calendar year, but prior to the
filing deadline of the annual statement, must report that interest
on the next statement filed by that official.


     I hope this has clarified the Act's disclosure requirements.
If you wish to discuss this, or need further information, please
feel free to call the Commission at (916) 322-5660.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Steven G. Churchwell
                                        General Counsel
                                        [signature]
                                        By: Jeanette E. Turvill
                                            Political Reform Consultant
                                            Technical Assistance Division

SGC/JET/jt
Enclosures


3/  Although positions in nonprofit entities are not reportable,
if any member receives income or gifts from such entities, the
member may be prohibited from participating in decisions which
have a material financial effect on the nonprofit entity.
Commission Regulation 18702.5 (copy enclosed) determines when a
decision will have a material financial effect on nonprofit
sources of income. If you need assistance in determining when a
member may have a conflict of interest, please feel free to
contact the Commission's Legal Division.
